title: 三只瞎老鼠
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\151-三只瞎老鼠-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
The narrative unfolds through several interconnected threads of mystery, deception, and retribution.

The story begins with Mr. Parker Pyne on Majorca, where he observes the tension between Mrs. Adela Chester and her son, Basil. Mrs. Chester is a conservative woman who disapproves of Basil’s relationship with Betty Gregg, a modern woman. Pyne intervenes to resolve the conflict, eventually discovering that the glamorous woman Basil has become infatuated with, Deloris Lamorna, is actually an actress named Madeline Salieri. Pyne orchestrates Deloris's departure from the island to break Basil's infatuation, which ultimately leads to a reconciliation between Basil, Betty, and his mother.

In a separate investigation at Litcham Manor, Hercule Poirot is called to examine the death of Hubert Litcham Rock. While the room is locked and a suicide note is present, Poirot rejects the suicide verdict. He discovers that the secretary, Jeffrey Keene, murdered Rock with a silencer and staged the scene to look like a suicide. Keene attempted to create a false timeline by firing a pistol from a window, but he was betrayed by footprints in a flower bed and a misplaced silk rosebud.

A third thread follows a dinner hosted by Barton Russell, intended to commemorate the death of his wife, Eileen. During the meal, Polly Weatherby is poisoned with cyanide. Poirot reveals that Russell himself was the killer; he poisoned Polly to secure her inheritance and attempted to frame Stephen Carter by planting a box of cyanide in his pocket.

The narrative then shifts to Mr. Satterthwaite, who encounters the enigmatic Mr. Quinn. Through their conversation, Satterthwaite reflects on the history of the Addison family at Doveton Kingsburn. At a tea party hosted by Tom Addison, a potential poisoning is narrowly averted when Satterthwaite realizes that Beryl Gillett has swapped teacups to exploit the color blindness of Timothy, a young man at the gathering. Satterthwaite deduces that Beryl intended to kill Timothy to secure a fortune for her son, Roland.

In another instance, a theft occurs involving the "Morning Star" diamond. Yvonne, a guest at a dinner hosted by Mr. Bohns, successfully executes a plan to steal the gem. However, Parker Pyne later reveals that the theft was a sophisticated deception carried out by the Amalfi family, who used play-doh and a fake diamond to facilitate the crime.

The final and most complex thread centers on Monkswell Manor, a guesthouse run by Molly and Jules Davis. The household is isolated by a blizzard and becomes the site of a targeted killing. A woman named Morine Gregg (alias Madame Lyon) has been murdered in London, and a notebook titled "Three Blind Mice" links the crime to a 1940 incident at Longridge Farm, where three children were involved in a tragedy. The residents of the manor—including the domineering Mrs. Boyle, the military man Major Metcalf, and the deserter Christopher Lane—all become suspects.

After Mrs. Boyle is strangled in her room, a man claiming to be Constable Trotter arrives to conduct an experiment to identify a liar among the guests. The tension peaks when the man reveals himself to be Jim, a survivor of the Longridge Farm abuse, who holds Molly at gunpoint for revenge. He is subdued by Major Metcalf, who is revealed to be working undercover with the real Detective Inspector Taner. The mystery concludes with the resolution of the Longridge Farm vendetta and the reconciliation of Molly and Jules, who realize they had both secretly traveled to London to buy anniversary gifts for one another.
